pages.name: csrf_1
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Step 1: Map the Target

elements.name: csrf_context
elements.html: The internal transfer portal at 192.168.1.55 lets authenticated employees initiate and approve transfers. If the portal doesn't properly verify that requests originate from legitimate user actions, you may be able to trick an authenticated user into submitting one on your behalf.

elements.name: csrf_recon
elements.title: How do you investigate whether the portal is vulnerable?

choices.text: Intercept a legitimate transfer request and inspect every field it contains [costs 2 min]
Browse the portal manually and look at how forms are structured [costs 1 min]
Examine the session cookies — check how they're scoped and whether they'd travel cross-origin [costs 1 min]
Run an automated scanner to flag missing protections [costs 3 min]

pages.name: csrf_1_cookie_branch
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Step 1.1: Cookie Analysis

elements.name: csrf_cookie_html
elements.html: The session cookie is set without a SameSite attribute and without the Secure flag. This means it will be sent along with cross-origin requests automatically. That's a necessary condition for CSRF — but you still need to confirm there's no server-side token validation on the transfer endpoint.

elements.name: csrf_cookie_choice
elements.title: Cookie analysis is promising. What do you do next?

choices.text: Intercept a transfer request to check whether a token is included in the body [costs 1 min]
You've seen enough — start building the payload now [costs 2 min]

pages.name: csrf_2
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Step 2: Build the Payload

elements.name: csrf_confirmed
elements.html: The transfer form has no unpredictable token — the request contains only the amount and destination account. The session cookie is not scoped to restrict cross-origin use. The portal will accept a forged request as long as it comes from an authenticated session. Now you need to craft something that triggers it.

elements.name: csrf_payload
elements.title: How do you build the forged request?

choices.text: Build a hidden form that submits automatically the moment a victim loads your page [costs 1 min]
Embed the request inside an image tag — quick and easy to hide [costs 2 min]
Use a script to fire the request programmatically in the background [costs 2 min]
Use an older async request method for broader browser compatibility [costs 2 min]

pages.name: csrf_branch_img
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Dead End: Image Tag

elements.name: csrf_img_fail
elements.html:
// DEAD END

The transfer endpoint only accepts POST requests — the image tag approach only works with GET. The payload fires but the server rejects it with a 405 Method Not Allowed. You've lost time and potentially tipped off any passive monitoring. You'll need to rebuild with something that can send a proper POST.

choices.text: Rebuild it as a hidden auto-submitting form [costs 2 min]
Rebuild it as a background script request [costs 2 min]

pages.name: csrf_2_fetch_dead
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Dead End: CORS Preflight

elements.name: csrf_fetch_dead_html
elements.html:
// SETBACK

The fetch() approach with credentials: 'include' triggers a CORS preflight OPTIONS request before the actual POST. The server's CORS policy rejects the preflight — the cross-origin POST never fires. The browser blocks it entirely. You need a method that doesn't trigger a preflight.

elements.name: csrf_fetch_dead_choice
elements.title: The fetch approach is blocked by CORS preflight. What do you do?

choices.text: Switch to a hidden auto-submit form — plain HTML forms don't trigger preflight [costs 1 min]
Abandon CSRF and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: csrf_3
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Step 3: Deliver the Payload

elements.name: csrf_payload_ready
elements.html: Your payload page is ready. When any authenticated employee loads it, a transfer will fire from their session without them knowing. Now you need to get the right person to open it — ideally someone with transfer approval authority who is currently logged in.

elements.name: csrf_delivery
elements.title: How do you get an authenticated user to trigger it?

choices.text: Send a targeted message to the CFO with a convincing pretext [costs 2 min]
Target someone in finance operations — they're likely logged into the portal constantly [costs 2 min]
Post the link to an internal message board where employees are active [costs 3 min]
Leave the payload hosted and wait for organic traffic [costs 5 min]

pages.name: csrf_delivery_cfo_dead
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Dead End: CFO Target

elements.name: csrf_cfo_dead_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

The CFO's email client renders messages in a sandboxed environment and strips external links before display. Your message arrived, but the link was replaced with a warning label. The CFO flagged it to IT security and your source address is now being investigated. You burned time and raised the alarm on the wrong target.

elements.name: csrf_cfo_dead_choice
elements.title: The CFO approach backfired. What do you do?

choices.text: Switch delivery method — target finance operations instead [costs 2 min penalty]
Abort CSRF and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: csrf_delivery_board_dead
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Dead End: Message Board

elements.name: csrf_board_dead_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

The internal message board previews links before posting — your URL was crawled by the platform's link-preview bot, which fired your payload against its own unauthenticated session and returned nothing useful. The post was then auto-flagged by a content filter for containing an external URL from an unfamiliar domain. An admin removed it within minutes.

elements.name: csrf_board_dead_choice
elements.title: The board approach was caught. What do you do?

choices.text: Switch delivery method — target finance operations directly [costs 2 min penalty]
Abort CSRF and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: csrf_4
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Step 4: Payload Triggered

elements.name: csrf_triggered
elements.html: A member of finance operations clicked the link while authenticated. The hidden form fired. A transfer request was submitted from their active session — they have no idea it happened. The amount is in the queue. But it requires a second approval before it clears. The window is narrow.

elements.name: csrf_final
elements.title: The transfer is pending a second approval. What do you do?

choices.text: Craft a second payload targeting the approval endpoint and deliver it to a different employee [costs 2 min]
Try to capture the session token from the employee who just fired your payload [costs 3 min]
Transfers auto-approve after a timeout — wait it out [costs 5 min]
Monitor the queue and cancel the transfer if it looks like it'll get flagged [costs 4 min]

pages.name: csrf_final_monitor_dead
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Dead End: Queue Monitoring

elements.name: csrf_monitor_dead_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

You don't have read access to the transfer queue — only the authenticated employee's session does, and that session has since expired. You have no visibility into whether the transfer is progressing or being reviewed. Monitoring passively from the outside yields nothing. Time passes. The transfer is flagged for manual review and frozen.

elements.name: csrf_monitor_dead_choice
elements.title: You can't see the queue. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and try a more active approach [costs 2 min penalty]
Abort CSRF and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: csrf_final_hijack_dead
pages.title: Path C — CSRF Attack | Dead End: Session Hijack

elements.name: csrf_hijack_dead_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

The session cookie is flagged HttpOnly — it cannot be read from JavaScript. Your CSRF payload already fired, but it had no mechanism to exfiltrate the cookie value. You have no way to reuse the session. The employee's session expired naturally a few minutes after they closed the tab. There is nothing to hijack.

elements.name: csrf_hijack_dead_choice
elements.title: Session hijack is not possible from here. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and try a different follow-through approach [costs 2 min penalty]
Abort CSRF and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

elements.name: csrf_win
elements.html:
// TRANSFER COMPLETE

An authenticated employee unknowingly submitted the transfer on your behalf. A second approval followed — either from another forged request or an automatic timeout. The transfer cleared.

Path C finished. Your time and decisions have been logged.
